items.prompt: An immutable array of 200,000 integers must answer 150,000 inclusive range-sum queries [l, r]. Which observation is the decisive signal for prefix accumulation?
constraints: The array does not change after preprocessing.
Queries may use arbitrary, non-monotonic boundaries.
The original index order must be preserved.

options.text: Each query can be expressed as the difference between two cumulative boundary states that can be precomputed once.
The values should be sorted so that every query becomes a contiguous interval in sorted order.
A single moving window can be advanced from one query interval to the next in constant time.
A frequency map of individual values is sufficient to recover the sum of any indexed interval.

feedback.reason: The reusable structure is a cumulative state at every index boundary. Arbitrary range sums then become a difference of two stored states.
feedback.details: Define P[i] as the sum of the first i elements. An inclusive query [l, r] becomes P[r + 1] - P[l], so one O(n) preprocessing pass supports each query in O(1). Sorting would destroy the meaning of the original indices, and a moving window does not provide constant-time jumps between unrelated query intervals. The same signal transfers to other invertible cumulative operations, such as XOR, but not directly to range minimum.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_before_queries: Sorting can make value-based searches easier, but it changes which elements belong to an index range. Prefix accumulation works in the original order and does not require sorted input.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.move_one_window: A moving window is efficient when boundaries evolve in a controlled scan. Arbitrary queries can jump backward or across the array, so maintaining one window would require potentially linear movement between queries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.store_value_frequency: A value-frequency map loses positional boundaries. It can answer global frequency questions, but it cannot identify which values lie between l and r without additional indexed state.

items.prompt: While scanning an array from left to right, which state is reusable for many later index-range sum queries?
constraints: The later queries are not known during the scan.
The query boundaries may have different lengths and may arrive in any order.

options.text: For every boundary i, store the sum of elements with indices smaller than i.
Store only the sum of the most recent fixed-size window.
Store cumulative sums after sorting the values.
Store only the largest element seen so far.

feedback.reason: A boundary-indexed prefix retains exactly the information needed to remove everything before a query start.
feedback.details: The invariant P[i] = A[0] + ... + A[i - 1] makes every stored state reusable across different query boundaries. A current window represents only one interval and discards older boundary states; a running maximum and a sorted prefix do not preserve the requested indexed sums. The state is cumulative rather than moving: it records history at all boundaries instead of maintaining one active interval.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.current_fixed_window: A fixed window is useful when every requested interval has the same size and the scan advances by one. It is not reusable for arbitrary starts, ends, or query order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_prefix: A prefix over sorted values answers questions about sorted positions, not the original array intervals. Sorting is a semantic change, not a prerequisite for prefix sums.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.running_maximum: A running maximum is not invertible into an interval sum. Subtracting two maxima does not remove the contribution before l.

items.prompt: Order the reasoning steps for deciding that prefix accumulation is appropriate for a static range-sum workload.
constraints: The sequence is a decision process, not implementation code.
Score is based on preserved adjacent relations.

elements.text: Identify that many queries repeat the same aggregate over different index ranges.
Define a cumulative state at each index boundary that summarizes everything before it.
Verify that the aggregate for one range can be isolated by combining its two boundary states.
Accept one preprocessing pass because it reduces every later query to constant work.

feedback.reason: Prefix accumulation is justified only after both reuse and a valid boundary-combination rule are established.
feedback.details: The workload first supplies the reuse signal. Next, the proposed boundary state must be defined, and then the author must verify that two states isolate the requested interval; only then does the preprocessing trade-off follow. Reversing this logic risks choosing a prefix merely because a problem mentions subarrays, even when the aggregate cannot be recovered from two prefixes.

items.prompt: A range-query design must support intervals that start at index 0 without a special branch. Which prefix-state convention best exposes the signal that the design is well formed?

options.text: Use P of length n + 1 with P[0] = 0 and P[i] equal to the sum of the first i elements.
Use P of length n with P[i] equal to the sum through A[i], but apply the same subtraction formula for every l.
Use a current window sum and reset it whenever l is 0.
Sort the input and use the first sorted value as the identity prefix.

feedback.reason: An explicit identity state before the first element makes the left boundary uniform, including l = 0.
feedback.details: With an exclusive n + 1 prefix, [l, r] is always P[r + 1] - P[l], and P[0] represents the empty prefix. An inclusive n-length prefix can also work, but it requires a different formula when l = 0 unless another sentinel is added. The decisive issue is not coding style: a convention determines which elements each boundary state contains and therefore which subtraction is legal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.inclusive_n_no_identity: An inclusive prefix is valid only with its matching boundary formula. Reusing the exclusive formula without an identity entry creates an off-by-one error, especially at l = 0.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_sentinel: Resetting a moving window does not create reusable states for arbitrary future boundaries. It handles one traversal state, not a range-query contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_identity: A data value is not the additive identity, and sorting changes interval membership. The empty prefix must contribute zero regardless of input values.

items.prompt: Which workload is the strongest signal for prefix accumulation rather than maintaining one moving window?
constraints: Only the stated output is required.

options.text: Answer 100,000 sum queries whose [l, r] boundaries arrive in arbitrary order.
Find the maximum sum among all subarrays of one fixed length k in a single left-to-right pass.
Find the longest window satisfying a monotonic frequency constraint while scanning once.
Find a pair in a sorted array by moving two endpoints according to the pair sum.

feedback.reason: Arbitrary repeated range queries reuse stored boundary states; a moving window is strongest when one scan updates one current interval.
feedback.details: A prefix pays O(n) once and then answers unrelated boundaries in O(1). The fixed-length maximum is naturally maintained by adding the entering value and removing the leaving value, while a monotonic validity problem is governed by expand/shrink logic. Prefixes and windows can both involve contiguous ranges, but their state ownership and query models are different.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_fixed_length_max: A prefix could compute every fixed-length sum, but a moving window achieves the same O(n) time with O(1) extra state. The workload does not need random range reuse.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.longest_positive_threshold: This task depends on maintaining and repairing one current validity interval. Precomputing sums does not by itself decide how to move the boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_two_ends: Two-pointer pair search uses ordered candidate elimination, not cumulative interval reuse. Prefix accumulation does not supply the movement proof.

items.prompt: An unsorted array contains positive and negative integers. It will receive many exact index-range sum queries and no updates. Does the prefix-accumulation signal still apply?
constraints: Queries ask for sums of original contiguous index ranges.

options.text: Yes. Cumulative addition follows original index order; neither sortedness nor non-negativity is required for range differences.
No. Prefix sums require nondecreasing values so that subtraction is valid.
Only if every value is nonnegative, because negative values make a prefix decrease.
No. Any problem with negative values must use a moving window instead.

feedback.reason: Prefix subtraction is an algebraic identity over the original sequence; prefixes may increase or decrease.
feedback.details: P[r + 1] contains the sum before l plus the requested range, so subtracting P[l] removes the earlier contribution regardless of signs or order. Sortedness matters for strategies that eliminate candidates by order, and non-negativity matters for some monotonic sliding windows, but neither is a prefix-sum precondition. Sorting would actually invalidate index-range semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_sort_required: Subtraction remains valid for any numeric order because both prefixes were formed from the same original sequence. Sorting is unrelated to the identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_if_positive: A prefix is allowed to decrease. Non-negativity is needed for certain monotonic expand/shrink proofs, not for cumulative sums.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_window: Negative values can break a simple monotonic window; they do not break prefix differences. In many exact-sum problems they make prefix-based state more useful.

items.prompt: For n immutable values and q arbitrary range-sum queries, select the total time and extra space of building a separate prefix array and answering every query from it.
constraints: The prefix array has n + 1 numeric entries.
Arithmetic on one stored entry is treated as O(1).
Input storage is not counted as extra space.

feedback.reason: The reusable state costs one linear build and one constant-time subtraction per query, while the stored boundary states occupy linear extra space.
feedback.details: The build visits n elements, and q queries each read two prefix entries, giving O(n + q) total time. A separate n + 1 prefix array is O(n) extra space. Prefix-hash variants also allocate auxiliary state—often O(n) in the worst case—so “prefix” does not imply zero additional memory.

items.prompt: Which new requirement preserves the same reusable-prefix decision boundary as static range sums?
constraints: The requirement must be answerable from two precomputed boundary states.

options.text: Answer many queries asking how many even values occur in [l, r], by prefixing a 0/1 indicator.
Answer arbitrary range medians by subtracting two scalar prefix sums.
Support point updates and immediately answer later sums using an unchanged static prefix.
Find the longest subarray with all distinct values by subtracting two prefix sums.

feedback.reason: A predicate count becomes an additive sequence, so two cumulative counts isolate the requested interval.
feedback.details: Map each value to 1 when it is even and 0 otherwise, then use the same P[r + 1] - P[l] rule. A scalar sum cannot recover a median or distinctness constraint, and online updates make a static prefix stale. The transfer rule is: derive a per-position contribution whose cumulative states can be combined to recover exactly the requested range result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.range_median_queries: Two scalar sums do not preserve the order statistics needed for a median. A richer data structure or offline method is required.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_point_updates: A static prefix bakes in every earlier value. After a point update, many later entries would need repair unless a dynamic structure is used.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.longest_unique_subarray: Uniqueness is a stateful validity condition, not an additive contribution that can be isolated by subtraction. A frequency-constrained window is the usual mechanism.
